topic: Coding Algorithms for Technical Interview Mastery
summary: A systematic, template-first course covering the 15–20 algorithm patterns, complexity analysis, and language-specific traps required for consistent FAANG-level coding interview performance.

modules.title: The Interview Algorithm Landscape
modules.strata_theme: Theme 3 + Theme 10: pattern frequency and empirically validated study sequence
modules.description: Understand the scope of what needs to be mastered, how community-derived study sequences work, and how to apply a structured communication framework during interviews.
objectives: Identify the 6 high-priority pattern families that cover the majority of FAANG interview problems
Apply the empirically validated study sequence from Arrays → Hashing → Trees → Graphs → DP
Use the UMPIRE framework to structure interview communication, emphasising the Match and Review steps

lessons.title: 15–20 Templates Cover 80%+ of FAANG Interview Problems
lessons.description: The Pareto principle of interview preparation — a small set of high-frequency patterns covers the vast majority of what FAANG companies actually test.

lessons.title: The Empirically Validated Study Sequence
lessons.description: How NeetCode 150, Blind 75, Grind 75, and TIH independently converge on the same dependency DAG — and why following that order minimises time to competence.

lessons.title: The UMPIRE Interview Communication Framework
lessons.description: A six-step scaffold — Understand, Match, Plan, Implement, Review, Evaluate — for communicating your problem-solving process; the Match and Review steps are most skipped and most valued.

modules.title: Hash Maps — The Universal Speed-Up
modules.strata_theme: Theme 8: hash map / set is the universal improve-time-with-space optimisation
modules.description: Master the hash map as the primary tool for eliminating O(n) scans and O(n²) nested loops — the single most widely applicable data structure in coding interviews.
objectives: Apply hash maps to convert O(n²) brute force to O(n) using complement lookup
Implement frequency counting, sliding-window counters, and index-as-key patterns
Distinguish O(1) average, O(n) worst-case, and bounded O(1) space scenarios

lessons.title: Hash Map and Set: The Interview Foundation
lessons.description: Why hash maps are the most commonly used data structure for time improvement, and the mechanics behind O(1) average-case performance.

lessons.title: Frequency Counting, Complement Lookup, and Index-as-Key
lessons.description: Three canonical hash map patterns: frequency counters for anagram detection, complement lookup for Two Sum, and using array indices as a hash map for cyclic-integer values.

lessons.title: Hash Map Language Traps — Python, Java, JavaScript
lessons.description: Language-specific hash map pitfalls: Java HashMap pre-JDK8 O(n) worst case vs JDK8+ O(log n), and JavaScript's silent Map-vs-object-property assignment bug.

modules.title: Linear Traversal Patterns — Arrays, Pointers, and Windows
modules.strata_theme: Theme 1: templates as primary unit — linear array patterns (two-pointer, sliding window, prefix sum)
modules.description: Master the template-based approach for the most frequent linear scan patterns: two pointers, sliding window, prefix sums, and the sort preprocessing heuristic.
objectives: Implement two-pointer convergence and fast/slow cycle-detection for pair-sum and list-cycle problems
Apply fixed-size and variable-size sliding window templates to substring and subarray problems
Use prefix sums to convert range-query problems from O(n) per query to O(1)
Apply the sort-preprocessing heuristic to identify when O(n log n) sort + O(n) scan beats O(n²) brute force

lessons.title: Two-Pointer Pattern — Four Canonical Variants
lessons.description: Left-right convergence, fast-slow cycle detection, same-direction expansion, and opposite-end split — four variants with recognition signals and worked examples.

lessons.title: Sliding Window — Fixed-Size and Variable-Size Templates
lessons.description: Expand-right/shrink-left for variable-size windows; fixed-window entry/expiry for sum/product problems; monotonic deque for sliding window maximum.

lessons.title: Prefix Sums — O(n) Build, O(1) Query
lessons.description: Build a prefix-sum array in O(n) to answer arbitrary range-sum queries in O(1); extension to 2D prefix sums for matrix subarray problems.

lessons.title: Sort Preprocessing — Unlock Binary Search and Two Pointers
lessons.description: Sort-first heuristic: when problem order is irrelevant, sort in O(n log n) to enable O(n) two-pointer or O(log n) binary search — converting O(n²) brute force to O(n log n) total.

modules.title: Tree and Graph Traversal — BFS/DFS Duality
modules.strata_theme: Theme 4: BFS and DFS are dual solutions — the same problem often has both
modules.description: Understand BFS and DFS as complementary, not competing, strategies and learn which problem type dictates which traversal on trees, graphs, and matrices.
objectives: Implement recursive and iterative DFS with three-colour cycle detection
Implement BFS with a deque for shortest-path and level-order traversal
Apply topological sort using both Kahn's BFS and DFS post-order reversal
Match flood fill, shortest distance, path existence, and dependency-ordering problems to the correct traversal

lessons.title: Tree Traversal Templates — Pre, In, Post, and Level-Order
lessons.description: The four canonical tree traversal templates: pre-order (root then children), in-order (BST sorted output), post-order (children before root), and level-order BFS.

lessons.title: BFS — Shortest Path, Level-Order, and Connected Components
lessons.description: BFS deque template, O(V+E) traversal, guarantee of shortest path in unweighted graphs, level-order enumeration, and the 2D matrix direction-tuple idiom.

lessons.title: DFS — Cycle Detection, Path Existence, and Recursive Decomposition
lessons.description: DFS recursive and iterative templates, three-coloring (white/gray/black) for directed-graph cycle detection, back-edge classification, and entry/exit timestamps.

lessons.title: Topological Sort — Kahn's BFS vs. DFS Post-Order
lessons.description: Both algorithms reach O(V+E); Kahn's BFS is preferred for explicit cycle detection (result size < V → cycle); DFS post-order reversal is preferred when DFS is already in use.

modules.title: Binary Search, Heaps, and Monotonic Stacks
modules.strata_theme: Theme 1: templates as primary unit — binary search, heap, monotonic stack
modules.description: Three patterns requiring distinct mental models: binary search for monotone decision spaces, heaps for priority-based selection, and monotonic stacks for next-greater-element problems.
objectives: Apply the unified binary search template to find leftmost/rightmost boundary in O(log n)
Implement the heap top-K pattern with a size-K min-heap in O(n log K)
Apply the monotonic stack while-violated-pop-then-push loop to NGE and histogram problems in O(n) amortized

lessons.title: Unified Binary Search Template — Three Modification Points
lessons.description: Single canonical template: while lo < hi, mid = lo + (hi-lo)//2; three variants for exact match, leftmost boundary, and rightmost boundary; rotated-array extension.

lessons.title: Heap and Top-K Pattern
lessons.description: heapify in O(n), push/pop in O(log n), and the top-K interview pattern: maintain a size-K min-heap to find the K largest elements in O(n log K).

lessons.title: Monotonic Stack — Next Greater Element in O(n) Amortized
lessons.description: The while-violated-pop-then-push loop that achieves O(n) amortized cost; applications to next-greater-element, largest rectangle in histogram, and trapping rain water.

modules.title: Complexity Analysis — Big-O, Master Theorem, and Amortized
modules.strata_theme: Claims C6, C7, C8: the formal complexity vocabulary FAANG interviewers expect
modules.description: Build the formal complexity vocabulary — from Big-O definition through Master Theorem mechanical derivation to amortized analysis proofs — that FAANG interviewers expect at mid and senior level.
objectives: Apply the formal Big-O definition to drop constants and identify dominant terms
Use the Master Theorem to derive complexities for divide-and-conquer recurrences in all three cases
Explain why dynamic array append is O(1) amortized using the geometric series argument
State the inverse-Ackermann O(α(n)) amortized cost for Union-Find with both optimisations

lessons.title: Big-O Formal Definition and the Growth Hierarchy
lessons.description: T(n) ∈ O(f(n)) defined with c and n₀; Big-Θ squeeze and Ω lower bound; canonical growth hierarchy O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n²) < O(2ⁿ) < O(n!).

lessons.title: Master Theorem — Mechanical Derivation for Divide-and-Conquer
lessons.description: T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n): three cases comparing f(n) vs n^(log_b a); worked derivations for merge sort, binary search, and Strassen; identifying inadmissible cases.

lessons.title: Amortized Analysis — Dynamic Arrays, Two-Stack Queue, Union-Find
lessons.description: Aggregate method for table doubling (total resize cost O(n) / n operations = O(1) amortized); accounting method for two-stack queue; O(α(n)) for DSU with path compression and union by rank.

modules.title: Space Complexity and Recursion Depth
modules.strata_theme: Theme 5: recursion depth is the space complexity for tree and graph traversal
modules.description: Understand how call-stack depth determines recursion space, the O(h) vs O(n) tree cases, Python's 1000-frame limit hazard, and when iterative alternatives are necessary.
objectives: Calculate recursion space as O(h) for balanced trees and O(n) for skewed trees
Recognise when Python's default 1000-frame recursion limit requires an iterative solution
Compare BFS space O(w) vs DFS space O(h) and identify which is preferable per problem type

lessons.title: Recursion Space = Call Stack Depth — O(h) Trees, O(V) Graphs
lessons.description: Each stack frame is O(1) space; maximum frame depth = recursion space; O(log n) for balanced trees, O(n) for skewed, O(V) worst case for general DFS.

lessons.title: Python Recursion Limit and the Iterative Stack Pattern
lessons.description: Python's 1000-frame default triggers RecursionError for deep trees; three options: iterative with explicit stack, sys.setrecursionlimit, or restructuring to tail-recursive form.

lessons.title: BFS vs DFS Space Trade-offs — Width vs Depth
lessons.description: BFS uses O(w) space where w = maximum level width (up to O(n) for complete binary trees); DFS uses O(h); for sparse graphs DFS often wins; for very wide shallow trees BFS wins.

modules.title: Dynamic Programming — The Three-Stage Pipeline
modules.strata_theme: Theme 7: DP has a systematic brute-force → top-down → bottom-up pipeline
modules.description: Learn DP via its canonical pipeline — not by memorising solutions but by systematically transforming brute-force recursion into memoised, then tabulated, then space-optimised solutions.
objectives: Identify optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems in a problem description
Add @functools.cache to a brute-force recursion and verify the time improvement
Convert a top-down memoised solution to bottom-up tabulation
Apply space optimisation by keeping only the previous row/column of the DP table

lessons.title: DP Recognition — Optimal Substructure and Overlapping Subproblems
lessons.description: The two structural properties that make DP applicable; contrast with greedy (suboptimal subproblems acceptable) and divide-and-conquer (non-overlapping subproblems).

lessons.title: Top-Down Memoisation — @functools.cache in Python
lessons.description: Add @functools.cache (Python 3.9+) to a recursive solution; verify O(2^n) → O(n) time reduction with Fibonacci; understand how unbounded LRU cache stores subproblem results.

lessons.title: Bottom-Up Tabulation and Space Optimisation
lessons.description: Convert top-down @cache to iterative bottom-up table; identify which previous states are actually needed; reduce O(n²) space to O(n) by keeping only the previous row.

lessons.title: DP State Design — The Hardest Step
lessons.description: The Markov property for DP state — state must capture exactly what is needed for optimal forward decisions; worked examples with Climbing Stairs, Coin Change, LCS, and 0/1 Knapsack.

modules.title: Language-Specific Complexity Traps
modules.strata_theme: Theme 2 + Theme 9: hidden O(n) costs and language-specific runtime details
modules.description: Systematic coverage of every hidden O(n) and correctness trap in Python, Java, and JavaScript — the operations that look O(1) but are not, and the correctness bugs they silently introduce.
objectives: Identify the 5 most common Python hidden-cost operations and their O(1) substitutes
Explain why result += char in a loop is O(n²) in Python, Java, and JavaScript
Describe the Java HashMap JDK7 vs JDK8 worst-case complexity change and its interview implications

lessons.title: Python Hidden O(n) Operations — list, deque, and Strings
lessons.description: list.insert(0,x), list.pop(0), slice in loop, string += in loop — each appears cheap but runs O(n) or O(n²); deque.popleft(), ''.join(parts), and pre-allocated lists as correct substitutes.

lessons.title: Java and JavaScript Complexity Pitfalls
lessons.description: Java: LinkedList.get(index) O(n), ArrayList.add(index) O(n), HashMap JDK7 O(n) worst → JDK8 O(log n); JS: Array.shift/unshift O(n), obj[key] silently fails to use Map.

lessons.title: String Concatenation — The Universal O(n²) Trap
lessons.description: Why result += char is O(n²) in Python, Java, and JavaScript due to immutable string semantics; the correct fix in each language; sorting algorithm trivia for bonus points.

modules.title: Backtracking, Union-Find, Debates, and Gaps
modules.strata_theme: Contradictions 1–2 + remaining templates (backtracking, DSU) + coverage gaps
modules.description: Complete the template set with backtracking and Union-Find, navigate the DP priority debate, and acknowledge the coverage gaps in linked lists, tries, and greedy algorithms.
objectives: Implement the backtracking template — explore, recurse, undo — for permutations, combinations, and N-queens
Use Union-Find for faster repeated-connectivity queries in Course Schedule-style problems
Apply the company-tier heuristic to decide how much DP investment your own prep warrants
Identify the three coverage gaps and the recommended resources to fill them

lessons.title: Backtracking Template — Explore, Recurse, Undo
lessons.description: The canonical scaffold: choose / recurse / unchoose; applied to permutations, combinations, subsets, and N-queens; pruning strategies to reduce the search tree.

lessons.title: Union-Find (DSU) — Path Compression and Union by Rank
lessons.description: DSU with both optimisations achieves O(α(n)) ≈ O(1) amortized; superior to repeated DFS for connectivity queries; template for detecting cycles in Course Schedule II-style problems.

lessons.title: The DP Priority Debate — General Prep vs. Google Track
lessons.description: TIH: 'DP low ROI except Google' vs. Safronov: 'DP is core'. The evidence resolves to a context-dependent recommendation: learn DP after high-priority patterns, not instead of them.

lessons.title: Coverage Gaps — Linked Lists, Tries, and Greedy
lessons.description: Three topics present in sources but not deeply covered: linked-list pointer manipulation (dummy head, fast/slow), Trie construction and prefix search, and the greedy exchange-argument criterion.
